[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books on magic these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book on the left is the current edition of my first published book, Paths of Wisdom., which originally saw print in 1996.  It's an instructional manual of the Cabala, one of the core philosophical systems of the Western occult tradition, in the form that I first learned -- the version taught and practiced in the Golden Dawn tradition. I intended it to be for beginners, but in those days I had somewhat expansive ideas of what beginners in magic could handle! Now I'd recommend starting with a simpler book such as my Learning Ritual Magic before going on to this one. (If you're interested, you can get a copy here if you live in the US and here if you're anywhere else.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

replies.title: Re: Mouni Sadhu's Concentration - First Exercise

comments.title: Longevity of the Astral Body
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

In my system (Gurdjieffian) the astral body gradually decomposes after death. It’s unspecified how long that takes. In that system, there is no expectation of a rebirth on the Earth.

My current hope or belief is that my astral body will eventually be of such quality that it will not decompose, at least not initially after death. I am hoping that life in the Astral World is a possibility.

I read a book by Papus, where he states, as well, that the astral body decomposes after death. And I believe the Hindus believe the same.

I gather from the Occult Philosophy Workbook that your system indicates the astral body is reincarnated from life to life.
Are you aware of any occultists that strive for a life after death in the Astral World?

Thank You!

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content:
Terms like "the astral world" get used in varying senses in different occult teachings, so let's cover some basics first. In the system I work with, there are seven planes of manifestation to which human souls potentially have access, and at this stage in our evolution we can only access four of them -- the material plane, the etheric plane, the astral plane, and the mental plane. The material plane is the world of matter and energy, the etheric plane is the world of life force, the astral plane is the world we experience in dreams and imagination, and the mental plane -- which we're just beginning to experience -- appears to us as meanings, intentions, and values. Incarnate humans have a material body, an etheric body, an astral body, and a mental sheath -- this latter isn't quite an independent body yet, which is why we only have glimpses of the mental plane. Okay? Now we can proceed.

Human souls reincarnate on the material plane, but they don't do so indefinitely. No, the astral body doesn't remain from life to life; the dying process involves shedding the material body first, then the etheric body, and then the astral body, in each case absorbing the experiences of each body into the soul. Then the soul descends through the planes, picking up a new astral, etheric, and material body for its next incarnation.

Once a soul succeeds in transforming its mental sheath to a mental body, however, it no longer incarnates on the material plane; furthermore, since the mental body is the first of the permanent bodies for our swarm of souls, once the mental body is formed, that's the end of death and rebirth for that soul. Once that's happened, the etheric body becomes the densest body the soul has, and it also has an astral body and a mental body. So it mostly experiences the etheric plane, just as you and I mostly experience the material plane, but the astral and mental planes can also be experienced.

So if you want to rise out of the cycle of death and rebirth, any system of sustained spiritual practice will do that -- that's what they're for. That won't get you to a life spent primarily on the astral, however. To do that will take long ages of further development, until you can form a spiritual sheath and then a spiritual body; at that point you no longer have an etheric body, your astral body is the densest body you have, and the astral plane is the one you primarily experience. (Rinse and repeat, and eventually you form a causal body, and your three bodies are mental, spiritual, and causal, surrounding the divine spark at the core of yourself -- but that's a very, very long ways from now for all of us, and we can't even imagine what that would be like.

Have I confused you thoroughly yet? ;-)

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content: Can you please define 'sustained spiritual pactice'? Is it the same as religious observance/belief? Sorry for yet another basic/stupid question.

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content: I use the term "spiritual practice" to include both religious and occult forms of practice. Thus you can get there by a regular practice of prayer and Scripture study, or you can get there by a regular practice of meditation, of you can get there by a regular practice of occult training, or by any other spiritual tradition. The crucial point is that it has to involve practice, not just belief. What ideology you accept means very little. What you do about it in your daily life means more -- and how you build a relationship with the power or powers you revere means everything.

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content: Thank you! And if I  understand you correctly, as a side effect of sustained spiritual practice, you could build defence/immunity/a shield against the darkness currently permeating the astral?

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content: Yes, very much so. That's one of the many benefits of regular spiritual practice.

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body

My eldest brother, who was in a nursing home, passed away suddenly during the summer. I arrived at the nursing home about an hour and a half after he passed so I could sit with his body for a while before it was sent to the funeral home. The lighting in his room was quite low and I was startled to see what looked like a flickering of light around his eyes and over his face, the rest of his body being covered. It was very subtle, almost more a movement than light but I know I wasn't imagining it. I can't see auras so I was startled that I was able to see this.

replies.content: At what point of development is a soul able to direct it's reincarnation consciously? I have a memory of you addressing that possibility some time in the past.

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content:

What’s your take?

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content:

I followed your explanation fairly well; you were quite clear.

You’ll have to forgive me if I bring up other systems, and even go my own way in figuring this all out.

I love going back to Plato’s world of ideas (though, I am no expert on Plato). His description seems so ideal; I am tempted to equate his world with either the astral or mental world.
I also currently apply the far Eastern doctrine of the three worlds — desire realm, form realm, and formless realm — to the material, astral, and mental worlds.
And same with Swedenborg’s Natural, Spiritual, and Celestial Heavens.

However, as far as the astral body goes, I really don’t want to wait a long time to have a permanent astral body. As Willy Wonka says, “I want it now!”

I am sure that if I allowed myself the opportunity to reincarnate I would find myself in a lower rebirth that I would be very unhappy with. In a sense, I choose for myself the very pessimistic Gurdjieff system. But it, like Tantric Buddhism, allows for the chance for perfection in just one lifetime.

replies.content: I trust you're aware that the universe is under no obligation to give you something just because you want it. replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Longevity of the Astral Body
replies.content:
Two points. First, your next personality won't know what your current personality knows, but your Individuality aka higher self knows all of it, and when you get to the point at which you're ready to move on past the wheel of life and death, those memories will start coming through. In Druid tradition, that's why Taliesin could say "I have been all things previously" -- after his initiation in the school of the bards (mythologized as "the womb of Ceridwen"), he remembered it all.

Second, you may not retain knowledge but you can certainly retain abilities, and pick up the knowledge very quickly last time around. How do you think Mozart got to be able to play the violin professionally as a small child? It's been suggested -- and with good reason -- that he was Antonio Vivaldi in his previous life, and had played music in many earlier lives as well. Clean up your karma and focus on magical practice, and you've got a very good chance of being born to parents who are enthusiastic occultists, and getting to where you are now by the time you hit your middle teens.

(Even if you don't manage that, the knowledge tends to seek you out. I had a lot of karma to clear off in this life, but I still found my way to magic by the time I was fifteen, and every tradition I worked with in previous lives ended up coming my way, sometimes via the most absurd of coincidences.)

comments.title: Occult “Ontology”
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer,

I am a practicing Christian but do really enjoy reading your blog and simply learning about magical theory. My question is about what the various levels or “ontology” of reality is from an occult perspective.

Something I find intriguing about Christianity is the fact that this topic is not expanded on much at all, despite all of the systematic theology the church has written on all sorts of various topics. You have the physical plane of existence, a couple spiritual planes (heaven/hell), some hints at a mental or mind plane, and that’s pretty much it. Nothing in between those are ever mentioned, yet it seems apparent to me that more exist.

But for magical systems there are obviously many more, and so I was wondering if you could expound on what all the various “planes” are, like astral or etheric because it’s all jumbled in my head! Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content:
Not JMG, but a (heterdox) Christian here.

You might consider looking into the writers of the Philokalia in the Orthodox tradition. They spell out quite explicitly the various aspects (the Nous/intellect, the passions, etc.), and these roughly correspond with Neoplatonic thinking on the matter (the latter of which is foundational for much of Western magical thinking, as I understand it).

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content: OP here, funny enough I am Eastern Orthodox but have not yet read the Philokalia and its authors. It sounds like exactly what I’m looking for. Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content: There is a good English translation in four volumes of the first four out of the five volumes of the Philokalia in the original Greek, by [Orthodox] bishop Kallistos Ware and others.

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content:
Certainly. The divisions are not as explicit and extensive as what JMG laid out below, but I suspect you will find correlations (the Desert Fathers were preoccupied with subduing the passions, which they associated with images or the imagination (astral?), and thereby achieving a more direct (image-less) contemplation (the mental?) and communion with the Most High). They would employ ascetic practices (material) to this end. St. Maximus, as distinct from some of the others writing on the subject, seems to suggest that it's not the imagination per se that is problematic, but the passions that are stirred by them. This glossary may be helpful.

Axé

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”

I am just finishing up that one, and it's taken me the better part of a year despite being fairly short! They're not the sort of thing you get much out of, just reading it straight through cover to cover, but it's been helpful to read just one saying at a time, read it again, think on it before sleep, come back to it if it still doesn't make sense...  etc.

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content:
Easily done. Let's take them from the bottom up. ("Bottom" and "up" here are of course metaphors -- the planes all occupy the same space.)

First there's the material plane, which is exactly what it sounds like, the plane of matter and energy as studied by physicists, etc. Your material body, the one that anatomists study, belongs to this plane.

Next is the etheric plane, which is the plane of life force, and is just as richly varied and complex as the material plane. You have an etheric body, which is the body that acupuncturists work with -- the meridians and the energy that flows along them are etheric, not material.

Next is the astral plane, which is the plane you experience every night in your dreams and every day whenever you imagine or remember something. You have an astral body, which our culture has taught you not to notice, and your perceptions of the astral plane are probably pretty vague and colorless due to neglect. They can be improved.

Next is the mental plane, which human souls can just barely perceive. You experience this plane when you understand meanings, values, and purposes. You have a mental sheath -- it's not yet a full mental body. In occult teaching, once you have a mental body you will not be reborn in matter.

Next is the spiritual plane. We don't have spiritual bodies yet. Religion is the standard way to call on beings who do exist and act on this plane, such as angels.

Next is the causal plane (that's causal, not casual, btw!) -- the plane of causes. We don't have causal bodies yet. Religion, again, is how we call on beings of this plane, such as archangels.

Highest of all is the divine plane. I don't imagine I have to tell you what's on this plane, and how to interact with Him...

Heaven and hell -- well, there we get into the differences between occultism and mainstream Christianity. From an occult perspective heaven and hell are temporary conditions after death and before rebirth into a new incarnation. Between lives, while experiencing the astral plane, you get to review the life you've just lived -- and you get to see it exactly as it was, without any confusions or obfuscations or lying to yourself. If you lived your life well, that's a really pleasant experience -- heavenly, you might even say. If you made a miserable mess of it, why, "hellish" is quite an adequate term. One way or another, it takes place on the astral plane.

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content:
That was very informative, thank you. Are there different sub-areas within the astral plane?

I know a guy, who when he was a child, could astral travel (I believe the term is) and enter the dreams of his friends who lived in his neighborhood. The next day he would be able to tell his friends what they dreamed of the previous night.

He stopped doing this because of other beings that noticed the vacancy in his own physical body, and was afraid that they could possess it if he didn’t “return home” first.

replies.title: Re: Occult “Ontology”
replies.content:
1) Yes, very much so. Each plane is divided into seven sub-planes or regions; for example, the material plane is divided into solids, liquids, gases, energy, and three higher sub-planes that appear to correspond to gravity, the weak atomic force, and the strong atomic force. The astral similarly has its own seven regions, three lower regions that are of the nature of substance, a middle region that's of the nature of energy, and three higher regions that are of the nature of space.

2) Many children bring through gifts like that from previous lives. If your friend took the time to learn protective rituals, he could probably resume the practice, and use it as a vehicle for spiritual healing.

comments.title: Healing
comments.content:
Dear JMG and commenters,

I would like to share a – hopefully – inspirational experience and ask a question.

The experience is a self-healing experience. In 2019 I started meditating and have been keeping the practice. This was an unexpected outcomes:

Since childhood I have suffered quite frequent headaches which often develop into migraine. In 2020 I had a headache and was deciding if it was the time to take a painkiller. Coincidentally, it was also the time when I usually meditated. I had an idea: I will try the meditation to heal the headache. I did. The headache ceased almost completely after about 20 minutes of meditation. Over the two years I have had many good results both with headaches and migraines – and the meditations proved even more effective than medication: headaches usually cease in about 10mins, migraines 15-25mins; it takes about 50mins for the medication to take effect-if it does. Over the couple of years my headaches and migraines got less frequent, too. However, if I am too exhausted to focus or be patient, I fail. I am happy about my results and would like to share the “healing” meditation:

1) Find a quiet place and sit in a comfortable position
2) Relax and gently close your eyes.
3) Focus on just being – all of you; which is, pain, but focusing on your pain has a curious effect – there is the pain and there is a distance between your consciousness and the pain. Focusing on the pain enables you to realise you are more than the pain; it lets you gently “touch” the border of the pain, gradually releasing the tight threads which cause the suffering
4) When your consciousness does not try to run away from the ache any more, in case of migraine there are two more things you can focus on: first, your breath. The pain rises or leaves a little while breathing; adjust your breath so it minimizes the pain and then gently go to the pattern 4x4.
A more difficult thing to focus on, which I have not mastered yet, is focusing on the slight movements of your eyes (this also influences the amount of pain). Very slight correcting movements can relieve a lot of pain; but it is very difficult (for me) to focus enough on these, as they are incredibly subtle.

Last Saturday, I tried a similar thing on my hurting knee. It needed much more focus, but to my surprise, it worked. And the effect was long; usually, when this pain wakes me up at night, I am very stiff in the morning and it takes quite a lot of exercise to be able to move freely. That morning, I felt very little pain and stiffness - and the feeling of lightness and flexibility lasted all day.

This is my experience. Now, the question. What is going on from the occult point of view?

Sorry for the length of my text and thank you.
Yours with deep regards,
Markéta

A form of building what you are for rather than fighting what you are against:
My son was having troubles with a scary image playing over and over in his head as he was trying to go to sleep. I told him instead of trying to fight it or stop seeing it, replace it - the next time it plays imagine the image getting brighter and brighter until you can't see it anymore, you just see white and imagine what is making the bright light (anything bright looking and feeling, whether it's a star, angel, god, goddess, glowing sword, sparkly winged unicorn.) replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You don't "unsee it," you mess with it. There are various ways to do this. For example, you can use parody and mockery -- laughter is an effective banishing, so if you turn the message into a raucous joke and snicker at it every time you see it, it loses all its power. You can also make it conscious -- if you regard the message as an attempt to manipulate and think of it in that context, it can't have its effect because it's no longer able to sink into your subconscious. Yes, you can also do both of these!

replies.content:
Incidentally, this can also work remarkably well for dealing with habits or addictions (I learned this from Tony Robbins, who was likely mostly teaching stuff that came from New Thought, I realize these days). I smoked when I was in the Army, and after I got out, I kept occasionally coming back to it. After reading about this technique, I called to mind a story an Army buddy told me about visiting Australia: he said that it was a thing for a tough guy Aussie to come up to you at the bar, lay his forearm alongside yours, and then put a lit cigarette in the valley formed between the two arms, and then play chicken to see who pulled his arm away first. I also called to mind a scene from the very silly adult cartoon show Clone High, where a dragon with an Australian accent says a lot of outrageously stereotypical Australian things and nothing else.

Putting these together, every time I found myself wanting to smoke, I imagined a big, tough Aussie coming at me with a lit cigarette shouting nonsense like "Oi! Oi! Wallabee! Vegemite! Koala!" and then putting the cigarette out on my arm (also imagining the pain as vividly as I could). This both made me laugh, which interrupted the craving and robbed it of its weight, and built a nice aversion to that same craving. It worked great for me.

Hope this finds someone who it can help!

two things I've found helpful:

1) Memorize stuff. Filling your head-space with something else, something new, that you actually need to work at to memorize, particularly music, crowds out everything else. Find something with lots of verses and commit it to memory. It was not my intent, but when I was learning to chant the psalms, that is what happened: every other ear-worm just shriveled up and died. Wasn't any room for them.

2) Sometimes when thoughts pester us-- no matter where they came from-- they are trivial, and we can just brush them aside and keep going. St. Paisios says thoughts are like airplanes: buzzing by all the time, but you don't have to build them an airport and let them land! But sometimes, if you want to really go for the gold, the desert fathers say... grab that thought by the throat! Hold it, examine it, find out why it has such a hold on you, why it was attracted to your airspace, and blow up the airstrip where it landed. Those suckers don't like being interrogated! When I've done this, I've learned ugly things about my own character, habits, and desires. But facing those things squarely, understanding how that created a point of attachment for the nasty thought to dig in, like a tick in a soft bit of flesh, and feeling a little sick to my stomach about it-- that is generally enough to detach the thing (and a deterrent to its fellows!). Prayer and repentance finish the job. Think of the icon of St. George, up on his horse with the long spear, pinning the ugly little dragon right through the throat ;) That little monster is your parasitic thought.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It is a lot to unpack, and you're supposed to do the unpacking -- that's what meditation is for, and meditation on your own experience is where you can find all the info you need.

To give you a push in the right direction, think of the planes of being you experience, and how you can affect them. You can't daydream your way through a brick wall -- daydreams belong to the astral plane, while the brick wall is on the material plane. If you want to get through the wall, you may start by imagining a way through, but then you have to take action on the material plane to put a door there.

In the same way, you can't change a problem with your self-image by doing things on the material plane. If you feel like a failure, it doesn't matter how many signs of material success you heap up around you, you'll still feel like a failure, because those feelings are on the astral plane and the material plane won't affect them. You have to work out the tangled emotions on the astral plane that keep you feeling that way, and then you can release the feeling.

Does that help?

A combo lesson from aikido and non-violent communication:
Focus first on keeping your balance and center, you don't need to be strong nor particularly well-defended if you are in-balance and aware.
You often gain more agency through receiving an attack than by blocking it. Verbally, find what you can agree with of what the attacker is saying and acknowledge it first; or at least (carefully) restate and sympathize with their perspective even if in disagreement over the issue. This is unbalancing to someone seeking/expecting resistance.
Focusing on hearing and receiving their attack (without being harmed by it) does double duty of informing you, so that whatever you offer in rebuttal is not premeditated, but tailored to the context.
Also, I am still surprised by the power of stillness, quiet, and being slow to respond.

I also believe it would be beneficial to do divination to determine their driving motivation; are they driven by religious righteousness, academic purity, thievery of your funding or spotlight? Whichever it is, that is the place they will likely overextend themselves and make mistakes that you can gently expose or redirect.

(I use terms like "gently" and "carefully" because, in my understanding, the less harm you do to them, the less you risk being harmed.)

Last week you suggested a love spell involving the symbolism of Netzach to make oneself more lovable. You also mentioned that the work would bring up all of one's "unlovable" traits as a consequence of the work.

replies.title: Re: Ethical love spells II
replies.content:
Excellent! That experience of doubt and unhappiness shows that you're already coming to grips with the unlovable aspects of yourself. If you knew what it was to be truly lovable, you'd be able to choose consciously to be that, and you wouldn't need magic.

As an adult, I’ve been learning about something I knew very little about: how the military actually say they want to think and act. Most of the U.S. Military’s doctrine and training manuals are available for free download by anyone, with only a few exceptions such as strategic nukes, Special Ops, current country assessments.

Official Doctrine is that the Commander’s Intent should be expressed in a single, memorable, present tense statement of the observable situation that, if observed would prove that We’ve Won. “Our forces control the bridge” is good, “Bridge bullies get their comeuppance these days” is not. "Our forces WILL control the bridge" suggests that right now, they don't. But that's not the observation that, if observed, would prove we've already won.

The difference between Current Situation and Commander's Intent shows what activities must go on the Task List and be assigned to those responsible to make it happen.

Remarkable how much this matches with the Statement of Intent advocated by JMG and Neville Goddard alike.

Seems that the U.S. Military has been most successful when it’s been obvious to them and everyone else exactly what simple fact on the ground would prove that they’ve won.

replies.title: What they say about Commander's Intent
replies.content:
Here are some examples of the way it’s described, from different branches of the military.

"The mission statement is a clear and concise statement (one simple sentence) of what the unit is assigned to accomplish. It expresses the unit’s primary task and purpose represented by the five Ws - When (time), Who (unit), What (task), Where, and Why (purpose) for the mission assigned. The task describes the action to be taken while the purpose describes the desired result of the action. Of the two, the purpose (Why) is predominant. The purpose of the mission statement is always represented by the words: In Order To."

"The commander’s initial intent describes the purpose of the operations, desired objective, military end state, and operational risks associated with the campaign or operation and describes the desired conditions in terms of behaviors needed to support enduring outcomes. It also includes where the commander will and will not accept risk during the operation. It organizes (prioritizes) desired conditions and the combinations of potential actions in time, space, and purpose."

"The clear and concise communication of commander’s intent—a personally developed expression of the purpose of the operation, the desired end state, and risk tolerance—is critical."

"Commander’s Intent describes how the Commander envisions the result at the conclusion of the mission. It shows what success looks like."

"Commanders delegate decisions to subordinate wherever possible, which minimizes detailed control and empowers subordinates’ initiative to make decisions based on the commander’s guidance rather than constant communications."

"The commander’s intent should specify the goals, priorities, acceptable risks, and limits of the operation. Subordinates should be able to operate independently for some period of time based solely on commander’s intent."

"The role of Commander’s Intent is to empower subordinates and guide their initiative and improvisation as they adapt the plan to the changing environment. Commander’s Intent empowers initiative, improvisation, and adaptation by providing guidance of what a successful conclusion looks like."

"The art of command resides in the commander’s ability to use situational leadership to maximize operational performance."

"The eight principles of Mission Command (Competence, Mutual trust, Shared understanding, Commander’s Intent, Mission Orders, Disciplined initiative, and Risk Acceptance) remain valid."

Mocha Amphibious Moose ready for deployment!

comments.title: Homework and Rite of Rays
comments.content:
Reporting back on homework: I asked before about concerns with Western European pantheons because of their link to violent and colonialist values that I don't want to embody. You reminded me that this is another form of faulty exceptionalism- both the error of thinking western gods are unique in their violence and that the west is unique in it's imperialist streak. I’ve been processing those biases and balancing my perspective with more research. I've let go of the western exceptionalism (and the correlated shame/guilt - hooray for OSA,) and can see the pulses of power and dominance in many cultures and many pantheons. I also see that a god or goddess embodying a value or force is beyond morality, and the stories often are two sided with complexity in how those forces come to bear (hooray for meditation!) Morality, however, seems to be something beyond a human construct- as your recent post about reincarnation points out, we are on a journey to learn lessons and eventually move beyond physical incarnation, and sleaziness is an obstacle to that (though what sleaziness comprises is also not a simple rule-set but dependent on context.)

I still feel grief at cultural tolerance for sleaziness, particularly when it results in losses of life for comfort, convenience, and profit and is supported by a system of morality that finds ways to justify that sleaziness. Yet, I now understand that my grief is not unique to my location or time; it likely existed in many other times and places, and is likely a reason why mystics and others who are spiritual aligned and ecologically-minded seem to reside at the physical and metaphorical edges of their societies. I interact with hundreds of people a day and am embedded in the society, so I'm having to find other coping mechanisms than retreat. Ironically, this includes connection with the Gods whom I was originally blaming; and blessing walks in which, ironically, I'm offering blessing to the members of society I am frustrated with. (It's amazing how the blessing walk reveals my own biases.) Thank you for the suggestion to reexamine.

A practical question: I'm still on break from the LRP in discernment on my reactions to it. I am realizing that converse to the strange reactions I had to the LRP, the period of Celtic Golden Dawn work was marked by increased wellness, focus, and drive in my daily life. I strongly resonated with the Rite of Rays. Do you believe the Rite of Rays from the CGD is appropriate as an opening for the SOP? -the initial visual and calling Mae is as normal, as is the first ray (alawn) coming down;
-then, on naming the second ray (plennydd), I see it come down my arm and create a polarity that inverts the first ray to come up from the earth;
-then, the third ray (gwron) comes down my arm and connects to the second ray and so becomes the receiving current of polarity which allows the first ray to be re-revealed as the virtuous middle. So the final image is of three rays the outer two creating a circle of energy going into and back out of the heart of the Earth, and the middle is a pillar of light from above (in total it appears like the power-on symbol you see on electronics.)
This is significantly different from SOP formulations in your DMH and WotGS books, yet I wonder if it contains the requisite elements.

Quick check: is it just me (entirely likely) or is anyone else having muddier and less consistent results from divination of late?

Thank you as always for this space and your counsel. May you and yours be blessed.

comments.title: Big music events: spiritually dangerous?
comments.content:
Hi JMG, do you think there are spiritual dangers in dance music subcultures? I've gone to festivals before and there are definitely some occult-aware people involved in putting them on and it's not something that's widely discussed in music communities - more of a "those who know, will know" type of thing. For example, Insomniac Events, who throw many of the largest music festivals like the Electric Daisy Carnival in Las Vegas, likes to add owl imagery to their festivals in a way that clearly mirrors the symbolism of Bohemian Grove, as well as all-seeing-eyes and other esoteric symbolism.

A guy I know told me his story about attending a "totally Satanic" dance party in Los Angeles where the venue was decked out with every kind of demonic imagery the producers could find. I asked about his impression of it, he saw it as just another aesthetic theme. I gulped and changed the subject.

Even without blatant signs of demonolatry, it seems likely that there are things going on at these events on levels beyond the mundane. It's an effective way to gather large amounts of energy with a common direction so it'd be more surprising if there weren't at least some organizers turning them to metaphysical ends. There are persistent rumors that Burning Man has an occult dark side that very few participants are informed of.

There are definitely some positive forces in the mix as well and many people involved have had uplifting experiences. The people in these subcultures assert a wide variety of spiritual views. Few are really serious about any path; there's a lot of dilettantism with people throwing dozens of religions and philosophies into a blender and picking out bits they find particularly aesthetic or "deep."

With that in mind, the events can be a lot of fun so could it be possible to take part while guarding against negative influences? Banishing rituals are a given, and developing subtle senses could help people detect the presence of malign influences and avoid them. Do you think that healthy participation with caution could be an option or is it best to just not get involved? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Big music events: spiritually dangerous?
replies.content: Much of what goes on at such events, and at rock concerts and similar venues, is magical in nature. Music creates emotional states, and the adulation of fans for stars generates a polarity relationship, so you've got a lot of energy flying back and forth. As far as I know there isn't any significant risk involved, though I'd avoid any event that uses a lot of demonic symbolism. You might also consider divination to guide you to events that will have, as we used to say back in the day, positive vibes.

replies.content:

Vigilant Citizen, web site obsessed with pop culture obsession with occult/satanist memes

comments.title: Addiction
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have a few questions bout addiction.

1) Are there spiritual or magical aspects of addictions? I've heard of people metaphorically referring to addiction as a demon. I've always dismissed this but maybe I shouldn't to so out of hand.

2) What deities would be good to pray to when trying to overcome an addiction?

3) Are there magical procedures that can be good to try to overcome addiction? I'm curious about approaches from both natural and ceremonial magic. It seems as though the etheric cleansing stuff like cold showers, fresh air, sunshine, etc may be helpful in this regard, but does addiction actually have anything to do with the etheric?

4) Not to do with addiction, but as a non Christian I was curious about how Jesus compares to figures from other religions. One of the features of Jesus that seems somewhat unique is the emphasis on forgiveness. In fact, it almost seems appropriate to characterize him as a deity of forgiveness (apologies to Christian monotheists). What do other people think of this? Are there entities in other religions with a similar emphasis on forgiveness? (The closest that comes to mind right now is Avalokitesvara, the bodhisattva of compassion).

replies.title: Re: Addiction
replies.content:
1) There can be, but in most cases an addiction is a way someone avoids dealing with something else in their lives -- which is why they cling to the addiction, even while consciously trying to get past it.

2) That depends very much on what the underlying issue is. For example, a person who's pursuing an addiction because they have creative talents that want to be expressed, but they've been convinced by family and schools that they can't or shouldn't do anything about that, will get best results from a deity of creativity such as Apollo. Someone whose addiction has a different root, though, will need some other kind of help.

3) Etheric strengthening practices are good for helping the body through the transition away from the addiction, but addictions are astral in nature -- that is to say, emotional and cognitive. Journaling to get down to the root issue is very useful here; the exercises of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which are a systematic form of journaling, can be especially helpful.

4) Forgiveness is only an important divine attribute in societies where guilt is a central theme. That's a minority habit among human societies; shame is much more common than guilt, and societies that focus on shame tend to invoke deities of purification rather than deities of forgiveness. Then there are the societies that don't fixate on either one, and the deities they invoke tend to have other interests entirely.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Was the Bavarian Illuminati influenced by older secret societies that had similar names (like Alumbrados in Spain and Roshani movement in Afghanistan)? If not, which secret societies influenced Bavarian Illuminati?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, that's one of the common traps that trip up researchers into Adam Weishaupt's secret society. The Bavarian Illuminati weren't occultists, they were hardcore rationalists, inspired by the French philosophes; today's rationalist skeptics are their equivalents and heirs. The one secret society that influenced them powerfully was Freemasonry -- they copied the Masonic system of lodges and degrees. The big difference was that you have to believe in a Supreme Being to become a Mason, and the Illuminati were atheists.

replies.title: Mandatory non-belief?

replies.title: Re: Mandatory non-belief?
replies.content: No, they were more secretive than that. They selected candidates for recruitment based on their beliefs, or lack of same, and vetted them further before telling them about, and then initiating them into, the higher degrees.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hmmm... But still, Manly P. Hall claims that there was an esoteric undercurrent beneath the surface of Illuminati, in his book "Masonic Orders of Fraternity".

Maybe, the Bavarian Illuminati might have used hardcore rationalism not as its ultimate goal, but as a means to undermine religious dogmatism of the Church, and replace it with ancient mystery teachings. This is akin to "nigredo" phase of alchemy, at the societal level.

As a similar example, Carbonari-type of organizations were also heavily invested in rationalism at the political level, but many of the high-ranking members of these organizations were also initiated in Egyptian Rite(s) of Freemasonry that were up to their eyeballs in occultism.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Maybe" is one of those eternally pointless arguments. Since it's impossible to prove a negative, you can believe pretty much anything you wish -- say, that the Illuminati were actually in contact with alien space opossums from Alpha Centauri. Prove me wrong!

I would encourage you to read the original Illuminati documents, the ones that were seized by the police when the Bavarian government cracked down on the order in 1786 and 1787. René Le Forestier's fine book on the order, now available in English translation, is also well worth reading.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
OK, I admit that it was a huge "maybe". But the use of scientific thought by occultists as a weapon against the Church is not a new thing. Occultists in the Renaissance Period did that as well. As another well-known example, the Royal Society used to be the "Invisible College" of the Rosicrucians.

And, what about Manly P. Hall's claims on the Illuminists?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The occultists had their own secret societies in 18th century Germany. The conservative occultists joined the Gold- und Rosenkreuz, while the liberal occultists joined the Strict Observance, and both fought against the Catholic Church at every opportunity -- the Rosicrucians tended to be Lutherans, while the Strict Observance was divided between followers of St. Martin and followers of German mystical writers. (They all liked Boehme, though.) It was a very lively time -- and both groups as well as the Illuminati tried to hijack as many Masonic lodges as they could! (You could do a very good rewrite of the fine old Steve Jackson game Illuminati! set in 18th century Germany, with the Gold- und Rosenkreuz, the Strict Observance, the Illuminati, and the Jesuits as the four conspiracies in the game.)

2) Hall was a brilliant writer and philosopher, but, um, he wasn't all that good as a historian, and he accepted claims in occult literature that a little study of historical works would have warned him against accepting.

comments.title: Theosophy
comments.content:
Greetings ADJMG,

What’s the relationship, if any, between theosophy and Golden Dawn?

replies.title: Re: Theosophy
replies.content: The Theosophical Society, founded in 1875, became the most important venue in the occult scene in Europe and the European diaspora by the mid-1880s. Many of the people who joined the Golden Dawn after it was founded in 1887 were Theosophists, and some of them -- including founded William Wynn Westcott -- were members of the Society's inner circle, the Esoteric Section. Despite this, the Golden Dawn had little in common with Theosophy -- by and large, it attracted people who weren't satisfied with what Theosophy had to offer, and wanted a more robust  and more Western approach to esoteric spirituality.

comments.title: About ancestral curses
comments.content:
My current partner is very interested in addressing, but seems to be a deep and vicious family curse. In terms of our own practices, she is fairly well versed in herbalism and natural magic, while I have been practicing the Heathen Golden Dawn. Is there anything within those traditions that you think could be useful in this project?

If not, what sort of professional help should we seek?

More generally, what is your take on ancestral curses and how they work?

replies.title: Re: About ancestral curses
replies.content:
That sort of thing can be very challenging. The first thing to do, in any case along these lines, is to figure out what exactly it is, where it began, and why. If it's a result of some nasty action on the part of an ancestor, and that can be undone or made good, that can solve the problem right there. If that's not possible, the next step is to figure out how the curse manifests and whether and how the current behavior of family members reinforces that -- this is extremely common. The third thing to do is to invoke one or more deities for help, if you're not already doing this.

The crucial point is that you can't fight a curse head on -- that simply reinforces it. You have to sidestep it if you can't just do something to resolve the imbalance that created it.

Blood type based diets.
